speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Ethel Katz, tape number two, October 2, 1995. So you're telling me about your father and his [NON-ENGLISH], his little-- his little flask of-- of-- of whiskey-- in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So he went to every child, to everybody. We have been [NON-ENGLISH]-- I have been, my two sisters, and the oldest sister with her husband, and the four children, and my younger sister, [NON-ENGLISH] with her little boy. So he went to everybody and gave him-- them a sip to-- to keep them alive, you know? This is stuck in my memory. When-- in-- yeah. Did I tell you that's-- then we left. My older sister was married-- [NON-ENGLISH] was married with a child. The other one, her boyfriend came up, and I went with them and my parents-- we all went together, though. And then we have been in the woods. You know that. After that, the Germans started to bomb our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe overnight-- that's all. We have to run. We went to another village, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we have been there a day or two-- I don't remember exactly. But yeah, my father and sister-- that's my sister-- has to get married. She cannot be in the same place with your boyfriend not married. So he made dominion. And they married there-- on the way. [SIGHS] Then we went to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How did you get there? Still with the wagon and walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still by foot-- by foot we went after the wagon, and our feet have been so sore. It was impossible to walk, but still, we were-- we have been stopping places. I told you, we-- we haven't been going all the time. We have been stopping for a day or a night. And the German comes after us-- we walk again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours would you say it was worth the walk you were on? How many miles did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? I-- I cannot tell you now. I know that's our feet have been-- we got to Palona. This was our destination. We were saying that's it. We cannot go anymore. We had to take off our shoes. We couldn't walk anymore in them. And we have been begging my brother-in-law, who was having the wagon with there, to come with us and my mother and father. And he was saying, no. We're not going. They will take us to somewhere where we're not going-- no. We staying here overnight, and we will see what we will do farther.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to say goodbye. My parents have been old people. They wouldn't go to the trains. They have been staying with them. And we, like, the two sisters with their husbands, and me, we went to the trains. It was trains-- cattle have been in the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where the trains was taking us, we-- we went. We didn't know where we going. We-- we have been planning. We will go to Kiev, but it's never happened. Instead to Kiev, the trains was taking us to-- near [NON-ENGLISH]. And then when we went in to this train, all of us sat. And I looked up-- my husband was sitting there. I met him the first time after-- I hadn't seen him before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he wasn't your husband yet, right? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. So we have been very happy. At least we got each other. And then we went with the trains. And they took us off to some-- you call [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, like a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: To farms, kibbutz, and they came from there with the farms to take us there. So we didn't know. I thought we going all together, but we couldn't sit in one wagon. So I packed for us. I have been sitting with my sisters and with the child in one wagon. They had been-- my husband was sitting in the other one, you know? All of a sudden, they took them to another village, [NON-ENGLISH]. I lost him again. [LAUGHS] I didn't know where he is. But after a while, you know, it was maybe six months or longer, one, from our city, a boy, was coming over from a village to change. They was having barter. They needed feed for field. We-- he was working with the grains. He was having grains.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we exchanged it. And then he saw us. And he was saying, do you know what? This and this with me in this village. Oh, when they find out, he came to us. And-- and then I guess they went over to our place already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then again, we have to wonder. It was all the time we have been-- we haven't been in one place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when are we now? This is around 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about the beginning, yeah. The-- the-- all the traveling was in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: --'41. You know, it was so fast. The German was taking those places. You couldn't form at one place, you know. If you have been already in deeper-- we went deeper and deeper to Russia, you know. [SIGHS] And then the last destination-- ours was Rostov, where we already in a [NON-ENGLISH] like how you saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: A farm or a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a farm. Yeah. We stop there. And this was a German colony. And when the Russian-- when it started the fight between Russian and the Germans, they took them away. They send them, or they left. I don't know. But all the little village was emptied-- there wasn't any German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they needed people. It-- this was already wintertime when we came. And all the crops have been standing in the fields not touched. So they sent us--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --to take up the crop. You know, we didn't have any clothes. We didn't have nothing, but we had to do it. Under the snow, we have to take out the grain. And when we started to work, they was giving us with-- with bread, with grain. This was our pay. It wasn't any money. You couldn't buy any place. You couldn't buy for money anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And 1942-- yeah, I have been keeping then my husband-- not my husband still-- my boyfriend. He was living in another place with his nephew. And I lived with my sisters, but we have been seeing each other. And then in-- they took him about 1943 in the middle, in May, or when they took him to the army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have been left there. But I got the good point. You know, they was bringing from Kiev-- when it was occupied Kiev-- all the kids, the Russian kids from the mayor-- majors sending their things. They organized like a orphanage, and they was having an instructor, a woman. And she was bringing them to this place where he lived. So I went there. And I got a job there, because I have been like a teacher. They was taking me in. So it wasn't bad for me. I was having that what to eat and to drink. And here I've been teaching the kids. In 1945, I think it was, when they already liberated Kiev, they were sending letters to bring back the kids to Kiev. So I-- it was having the instructor, which one was bringing them-- liked me very much. And she was saying, you know what? You go with me to Kiev. This would be your only chance to get out. With that, we will-- will always living in this village. We won't have a chance to go any other place. So you were in that village for about two years is what you're saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1945. Then I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: When in-- when in 1945?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when-- what month in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. It must have been in summertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, saw my sisters, my two sisters. They-- they have been happy. Maybe they will have a chance-- when I'll go out, I will be able to take them out, too, from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went. And the way going there, they took my all papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] They got all of them. [LAUGHS] You know that it was very dangerous then. If you was having some papers, they was watching just to get them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came to this city, to Kiev, without papers. Can you imagine? They would arrest me right away. But thanks to this woman, she was having-- she went to her husband's brother. And he was in the police bureau, you know? He was a big [NON-ENGLISH]. So he got me papers. And I lived with them for a while. You know, they gave you a little room-- a room. It's only the bed was standing. That's all what it was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I-- if she went to sleep-- we have been sleeping in the same bed-- if she went to bed first, I have to wait till she will go in. It wasn't room for both of us to stay there. And I went to work to this orphanage there. While I have been going to work-- it was across the street-- always I have been-- I have been seeing a old woman sitting on a bench, and she watched me. How many times I went through, she watched.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once she stopped me and was asking me. Listen. Are you a Jewish girl? I was saying, yes. So what are you doing there with the Christian people? Come to live with me. I have a big house. I am all alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was with one daughter. And she was having three sons on the front-- they have been all majors. Come and live with me. I won't charge you anything. Just live with us. I like you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was saying, well, I have to talk to her, because I felt I am obligated.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] They have been happy that I could go, because they didn't have any place for me anyways. So I talked it over with my-- my girlfriend. And-- and I went to live there. Meanwhile, I was having letters. My husband was on the front. It's only-- what kind of letter I was having-- two words-- I'm alive. And this what kept me going. I could have a chance to go to the States with boys-- some. But I was saying no. I am waiting for my husband to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1946, he wrote me a letter that he's coming home to me. And I have been working in this day. And he came up. And-- and the girls, the teachers, jumped out to me-- I have been with the kids outside on the garden giving them a lesson.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they was saying, look! Your boyfriend is here. I didn't believe them, because it was a couple of times they teased me. [LAUGHS] And no one was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was saying, 'Go away.' And then I looked up in the road. He was coming up-- in the uniform, with the boots, high boots, shiny by high boots, with a nice [NON-ENGLISH], you know? So-- A uniform. Yeah-- yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uniform, right. I am mixing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. It's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I'm not-- I'm talking Jewish, and German, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --and Russian-- any way you want. Anyways, they took him in, and gave him a cup of coffee, and let him to wash up. And then they-- they give gave him a chair outside. And they took all the kids around him, and they made him to tell all the story where he had been, what he had been doing, how-- everything there. Then they gave me like two hours before quitting. She was saying, now go home. And we went home to this old woman. We had been calling her babushka. That means grandmother. She didn't let him sleep over. He was-- she was saying, 'Well, now go and take your license.' It wasn't-- it wasn't any Rabbis or things like to have a wedding-- this forget. And I have been only the Jewish girl where I have been working. But anyways, we went to the City Hall, and we got a license.

speaker: interviewer
text: We came home in the evening from the orphanage. They came-- all the workers, you know? We took our [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, our harmonica. Harmonica.

speaker: interviewee
text: Harmonica. And they started to play-- and-- and with food. And they made for-- for us, the wedding. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In the house of my-- of the grandmother, no? This was my wedding. And thank God, next May would be 50 years we married.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So well, what else you-- I'd like to ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --maybe I missed out a lot. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. I want to ask you, did you ever try to get back your property-- what had been your family's property-- in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. We went-- we lived-- when he came from the army, we lived there and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah. We lived there in Kiev a couple of years. My older son was born there. And he was born premature.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tevia-- Tom, you can call him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when? What year was he born? And what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947. We got married in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the 31st of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was Tevia born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born the 14th of March-- the 17-- I'm sorry. The 14, Harold was. The 17 of March, 1947. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So after-- maybe he was two years old or three-- two years, yeah-- we went-- we wanted to see the city, how it looks, maybe something left from our houses from-- anything left-- maybe some people we know. Maybe they would know what's happened to-- to ours, because it was left-- not all my family went.

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother-- the oldest brother went with us. The second they took right away in the army, he got-- got killed right away. The third one was left. We haven't seen him. I didn't know what's happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we wanted to know if we can find out anything about them. So we went to the city, to Ostrog-- back. It wasn't left anything-- not from my sister's houses-- nothing. But what I wanted to have something to remember. Can I stand up to show you? You'll show us after.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- something to remember. We went to the gentiles who lived around there in the outskirt to beg them if we can find something from our house just to have it to remember from. So I got those [NON-ENGLISH]. You know how you say the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Little cups-- little kiddish cups.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kiddish cups, yeah-- a couple of them. And what else I got? A ladle, a silver ladle.

speaker: interviewer
text: A ladle, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: A ladle. And this-- yeah-- this is about it what we got from them. I paid them good. I was saying, 'I'll pay you. How much you want?' But I want to have something from my family to remember. We left everything-- only the keys we got when we left from the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what kind of thoughts you had when you were going through all these experiences? When you're off in Kiev and wherever, and even when you were running-- what was going through your mind? Did you think you would make it through the war? Did you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we didn't. We didn't think we'll make it. But we run-- we even didn't know where we're running. We have been running like sheeps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, which of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Our destination was we going to Kiev, because Kiev in our mind was-- it's a big city. And it's a Jewish-- lots of Jewish live there. But it's never happened. Instead to kill, we went to-- near [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which of your family members survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It survived only my older sister, [NON-ENGLISH]. Like we have been three sisters after the brothers. No one from my brothers survived. No one from all the sister from the other mother survived-- and her kids. It's only us who-- who went with the trains, who have been left. In Polona, I told you, they was saying they will go next day with the horses farther. They-- they started to bomb. When we left, they started to bomb-- nothing left of the city at all there. Everyone was killed who was there. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You simply-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So yeah. You wanted to know about my sisters. So my oldest sister, [NON-ENGLISH], and her husband with a little boy have been-- we have been all the time together-- and my younger sister with her husband. And it was born-- my older sister was born another child during the war, a girl. And the other sister was having a-- a boy, and a son and a daughter. They live in Israel. And my older sister lived in Canada. I didn't tell you how after the war, how they got. Maybe my husband did tell you. [SIGHS] When I went to Kiev, I have been working there. And my mind was-- I will take them out from this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it's happened in 1945 that-- that people could emigrate already. They could go places, you know? So they sent me a letter to Kiev that's they going out of this place-- if I want to meet them in Kiev. Their-- their trains will go through. They will stop there, and we will meet. But we never met.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was saying, I'm not going with you, because I'm waiting. We-- I promised him I'll wait. So I have to wait. So they left. And this was right after the war in 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: 40-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm rushing myself. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left. And they organized the places for those people, for those all emigrants. And they could choose to go up to Israel or to Canada, you know? So my older sister, like I told you, we was having one sister in the States, which one emigrate. I never knew when-- before the war-- first war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and she send-- she wanted to go to-- to the States, but she couldn't go, because they didn't take in. They was taking into Canada, not to the States. So they went to Canada. My other sister, her husband was having sisters in Israel living from before the war there. So he was saying, I'm going to Israel. So they-- one went to Israel, the other went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is the way. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --so how they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have the most beautiful grandchildren-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: --like every grandmother. They are-- How many do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have four grandchildren. My oldest son is having one girl-- Haley, is her name. And she is-- like the first of October, yesterday, it was her birthday-- one years old; and a grandson, one years old from the other son. She was born three months. She is three months older than the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: --grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ari.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's two others. There's--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then is Jordan, and then is Jonathan. It was just in April we was having Jonathan's bar mitzvah. So the three-- his three sons already went through bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you worked here in Toronto since you came to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. My husband, when we came to Toronto, we lived with my sister a couple of months. And I was saying, we have to find a place for ourselves. So we rented a flat. We lived there-- I don't know how long-- not too long, because they didn't let the kids to bring in any other kids-- you know-- friends or any.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told my sister. And she was saying, you know what? Come and live with us again for a while. And then when you will be able to find another place where you can be free, you'll go. So we went back to her. We lived with her another-- maybe a 1/2 a year. And we found another flat, which one was OK. We lived there till-- till we got a house. We lived there quite a few years-- maybe 10 years. I don't remember exactly. Have you worked here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the first couple of months or a year, or-- I didn't work. I have been at home. My sister used to work. Everyone went to work, and I have been in the house preparing supper, you know. My husband got his job right away, so he worked. After that-- so I went in the evenings-- I went to learn a bit English. When I just got the basics a little bit. Because we came to Canada, I didn't know not one word of English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a chance, you know. My watch stopped. And my sister told me, you know what? You go over to Simpsons. And they will fix it. There is a good man. He-- he doesn't take too much. They will fix you the watch. So I went up till I find this place. It wasn't easy for me. I didn't write much. I didn't have money. And I didn't go nowhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, I found this place. And-- and he told me, 'You come tomorrow. You'll pick up your watch.' I was saying, 'I cannot come tomorrow.' [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't have money to-- to spend again for tickets, you know? So he was saying, 'If you can wait an hour or two, I'll fix it for you.' So I was saying, 'I'll wait.' Meanwhile, I didn't have what to do. I went up and down by the escalators. While I went up, I have seen a sign-- employment. So I was saying, 'What can I lose?'

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I'll go in and ask. Maybe they have a job for me. I went in. I still, my-- my time wasn't saying--

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I could read.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when-- when you know other languages, you pick it up. You-- you look at it, and you will know what this means. But to write and to-- to say it, I couldn't. [LAUGHS] Anyways, they was having some Polish people working them. So they was asking me what I want. I told them if I could find a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was saying, 'You know, I think you are lucky. Can you do something with a needle? But it needs very fine work. Of course, we have a department what they're selling the-- the leather gloves, the fine, very fine imported French kid gloves. They are sometimes missing a little button when it comes imported from there-- or a stitch, or--' 'Oh, yes,' I was saying. 'I-- I can do that.'

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And they hired me. They were saying it was before Christmas time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you indeed have that skill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could-- I have been doing at home embroidery some, and I-- I have been good with a needle-- fine things to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked there for a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Simpsons, I worked for 18 years-- close to that. I-- I could have worked longer. But my husband-- after 18 years, I worked. He retired by 65. I haven't been 65, but he retired. I thought it's time for me to quit too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was saying, they-- they have been shocked. How can you quit? My customers, they loved me. How can you quit? I have been awarded there a couple of times for good work, you know. They liked me very much.
